full_response:
Let us label two convenient inertial frames.  

S₁ : the rest frame of Knight #1 (his horse and his starting line are at rest here).  
S₂ : the rest frame of Knight #2 (this is the frame in which we want to know the motion of Knight #1’s starting line).

Given data (Arthur’s rules of the race)  
•  Knight #2 moves with speed v relative to Knight #1.  
  ⇒  In S₁ the 4-velocity of Knight #2 (and of everything attached to him) has spatial component +v along the x–direction.

We want the x–component of the velocity of Knight #1’s starting line as measured in S₂.  
In S₁ that starting line is at rest, so

 (u′)ˣ  = 0  (in S₁).

To transform this velocity to S₂ we use the special–relativistic velocity-addition formula (with c = 1):

 uˣ  =  ( (u′)ˣ + v ) / ( 1 + (u′)ˣ v )
     =  ( 0 + v ) / ( 1 + 0 · v )
     =  v .

The positive sign tells us the line moves toward Knight #2 from the −x direction, i.e. it approaches him head-on with speed v.

Therefore, according to Knight #2, Knight #1’s starting line approaches him with exactly the same speed v that he has relative to Knight #1.

\boxed{v}

Confidence: 65%
